comments.body:
[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with this child]

Reviewed the 7 cross-child interaction modes for this child against the whole sibling roster (S1 6d56, S2 70c7, S3 fa49, S5 b7fa, S6 1ba5). One finding (dependency gap); the rest are clean.

FINDING (dependency gap — snapshot handle not delivered to the plan-review claim-gate path)
- criteria: this child AC4 ("Plan-review's file_impact hashes and the claim-gate freshness re-check use the same pinned-SHA ref basis (no whole-HEAD divergence)"); parent AC1 ("all read a faithful snapshot materialized at the pinned SHA") and AC2 (signed verdict reproducible).
- location: this child (30b3) AC4 + Scope ("Out: gate parameter threading (S3)"); S3 fa49 "What"/Scope/file_impact.
- evidence:
  1. The claim-gate freshness re-check AND the signed dependency map are both computed from the SERVER WORKING TREE today: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:106-107 (dependency_hashes hashes paths from _config.repo_root(...)) and attest.py:325-326 (the ADR-0002 freshness re-check re-hashes from _config.repo_root). Neither reads a snapshot dir.
  2. Re-rooting gate code-reading to the snapshot is S3's job, but S3 (fa49) scopes its re-rooting to the LLM file tools only ("point the gate's file tools (pai_tools root / cfg.repo_path) at the snapshot dir") and its file_impact lists completion.py / pai_tools.py / mcp_server.py — NOT plan_review/attest.py.
  3. This child's file_impact lists transition.py + completion.py — also NOT attest.py — yet AC4 (the attest.py hashing path) is in this child's "In" scope while it pushes "gate parameter threading (S3)" out of scope.
  4. No sibling in the roster lists attest.py in file_impact (confirmed by search); so the snapshot-handle/pinned-SHA hand-off INTO the plan-review attestation hashing path is owned by neither S3 nor explicitly by this child.
- scenarios: attested plan-review signs file_impact hashes taken from the snapshot at the pinned SHA, but the claim-gate freshness re-check (run later) re-hashes the same paths from the server working tree (possibly a different branch / dirty) → spurious "stale-code" drift or, worse, a re-hash that silently passes against the wrong branch — re-introducing exactly the moving-checkout false-verdict the parent epic exists to kill. AC4's "no whole-HEAD divergence" cannot be met if attest.py still resolves through _config.repo_root.
- impact: AC4 (ref-coherence) and parent AC1/AC2 (snapshot-only reads, reproducibility) are unsatisfiable as decomposed: the resolved-SHA snapshot handle is not routed into attest.py's dependency_hashes() / claim-gate re-check, and the responsibility for routing it sits in a seam between this child and S3 that neither claims.
- checklist_item: dependency gap (cross-child mode 3)
- suggested_fix: Make the snapshot-handle/pinned-SHA basis an explicit hand-off: either (a) extend S3's re-rooting scope to include src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py (dependency_hashes + the ADR-0002 freshness re-check), threading the snapshot dir as the hash base in place of _config.repo_root, and list attest.py in S3's file_impact; or (b) add attest.py to THIS child's file_impact and an AC making "dependency_hashes() and the freshness re-check hash from the pinned-SHA snapshot, not _config.repo_root" explicit, since AC4 already claims claim-gate ref-coherence as in-scope here.

Clean on the other 6 modes:
- implicit shared state: verified_at_sha (resolved by S1, threaded by S3, consumed here, surfaced by S5) — consistent producer→consumer chain.
- conflicting assumptions: local-mode unsigned (S3 flags / this child bars / S5 reflects unsigned) and attested-default-still-signs (S5 AC) agree.
- scope overlap: local-mode split (S3 marks-unsigned vs this child enforces-the-bar) is clean, no double-implementation.
- ordering violation: logical S1→S3→this-child→S5 ordering; no contradiction (all tickets carry empty deps uniformly; parent AC8 only requires all-closed+green).
- consumer impact: S5 surfaces verified_at_sha; S6 documents in-toto shape + HMAC limits (this child delegates docs to S6, covered by S6 AC4).
- residual references: --force-close signal, ADR 0002, S6 all resolve.

- Parent AC2 (signed verdict records verified_at_sha + reproducible re-run over identical content) — FULLY covered by THIS child (its AC1: attested PASS records verified_at_sha on the ticket, signs over it, re-run reproduces a verdict over identical content), with the immutable-SHA basis from S1. This is the heart of this child. Clean.
- Parent AC3 (local mode reads in-place checkout AND cannot produce a signed verdict) — the "cannot sign" half FULLY covered by THIS child (its AC2: a local/dirty run never emits a signature, verify-and-report only); the "reads in-place checkout" half covered by S3 (its AC2). Split is clean; no uncovered half.
- Parent AC4 (concurrent gates, no git-lock contention / cross-op corruption; atomic population; delete-on-last-close) — FULLY covered by S1 (atomic population) + S2 (single-flight, delete-on-last-close). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child; claims none. Not an absence.
- Parent AC5 (snapshot cache + janitor reclaim under pressure without disrupting in-flight reads) — FULLY covered by S2. STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child. Not an absence.
- Parent AC6 (private-repo fetch failure surfaces a descriptive, actionable credential error) — FULLY covered by S1 (raises) + S6 (documents). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child. Not an absence.
- Parent AC7 (portable: no external tar, no hardcoded /tmp, flock+mkdir fallback) — FULLY covered by S1 (implementation) + S6 (docs). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child. Not an absence.
- Parent AC8 (all six child stories closed; make lint + full test suite green) — STRUCTURAL/meta epic-completion criterion; covered by existence of all six children (this child is story #4 of 6 in the roster) + each child's own green-bar obligation. Not an absence at this child.

Success-criteria narrative (every gate verifies a client-specified immutable reproducible snapshot; signed verdicts reproducible + bound to verified_at_sha; unsigned local preserves verify-before-push) decomposes onto AC1-AC3. The "signed verdicts are reproducible and bound to verified_at_sha" clause is precisely this child's core deliverable (its AC1/AC3, in-toto Statement envelope) and the "unsigned local preserves verify-before-push" clause is reinforced by this child's signing bar (its AC2). No uncovered obligation introduced.

Note: this child's AC5 (HMAC trust-model docs) is explicitly delegated to S6 ("delivered via S6"); S6 AC4 names that obligation — covered-by-named-consumer, not an absence.

Note (scope, not a G3 finding): the existing G4 review on this child flags a seam/dependency-gap around attest.py routing between S3 and this child. That is a consistency (G4) concern about WHO threads the snapshot handle into the plan-review hashing path — not a G3 coverage gap, since parent AC2/AC4-coherence is still CLAIMED by a child (this one). G3 union-coverage of the parent criteria is complete.

Conclusion: this child cleanly covers the signing/attestation slice of the parent criteria; no parent acceptance/success criterion is left uncovered by the roster. No G3 finding for this pairing.

- Parent AC5 (private-repo fetch failure surfaces a descriptive, actionable credential error) — FULLY by S1 (raises the actionable error) + S6 (documents message + remedy). (ADR 0002 — the code-drift ADR — is a DISTINCT artifact owned by S4b e308; not to be confused with the architecture ADR of parent AC8.)

The "never the server's mutable checkout / immutable pinned-SHA basis" clause is S1/S3; "local preserves verify-before-push" is S3's in-place read + THIS child's signing bar (AC4). No uncovered obligation introduced by this child's existence.

Named-consumer / delegation cross-checks (covered-by-named-consumer, not absences):
- This child's Scope explicitly delegates the claim-gate ref-resolution coherence OUT to sibling S4b (e308) ("Out: claim-gate ref-resolution coherence (sibling story)"). S4b IS present in the roster and CLAIMS it (e308 AC1: plan-review file_impact hashing + claim-gate freshness re-check resolve through ONE shared boundary at the same pinned-SHA basis). The reproducibility sub-obligation of parent AC2 that depends on the signed dependency map and the later freshness re-check hashing at the SAME basis is therefore covered by a named sibling — not orphaned.
- snapshot/cache (S1/S2) delegated out and owned by S1/S2/S2b; HMAC-trust-model docs delegated to S6 ("limits documented in S6"), claimed by S6 AC4.

Note (scope, NOT a G3 finding): the standing G4 dependency-gap on THIS child (the three sign/verify-side hashing sites — attest.py dependency_hashes()/_rehash()/the ADR-0002 freshness re-check — rooting on _config.repo_root, with neither this child's nor S4b's file_impact naming src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py) is a CONSISTENCY (G4) concern about WHICH module the manifest pin + shared resolution boundary actually land in and WHO routes the snapshot handle there. It is NOT a G3 union-coverage absence: the manifest-channel SHA-binding obligation IS claimed by THIS child (AC1/AC2) and the ref-coherence obligation IS claimed by S4b (AC1). A criterion that is claimed by a child is covered for G3 purposes; the where-does-it-land question belongs in the G4 channel for this pairing.

ANTI-FP cross-check on the one plausible absence: could parent AC2's "reproducible against that SHA" be left UNCOVERED because this child binds the SHA in the manifest but the verdict body might still be computed against the mutable checkout? Checked the whole roster — NO: the snapshot-only read basis is claimed by S1+S3 (AC1 re-rooting + the AC7 wrong-branch-invariance regression test), and the residual attestation-hashing/ref-coherence path is claimed by S4b (AC1) with a negative test guarding whole-HEAD divergence. The obligation is claimed across the roster; the only open question (which module the routing lands in) is G4, not a G3 absence.

Conclusion: this child cleanly covers the attested-signing slice — it is the PRIMARY/SOLE OWNER of parent AC2 (manifest-channel verified_at_sha binding, no payload bump, prior signatures verify, reproducible), the signing-bar half of AC3, and the payload-schema-SHA-binding rejected-alternative clause of AC8; and it contributes its own signing-side concurrency safety (CAS pin) and fail-closed resolution boundary. compiled_state.description:
# Attested signing: pin verified_at_sha via the signed manifest channel

## Why

A signed verdict is only worth anything if it is reproducible and bound to specific content.
Today the verdict signs a manifest and stores git `head_sha` as AUDIT METADATA ONLY
(signing.py) — nothing cryptographically pins WHAT code was verified. Attested mode must
resolve->pin->record->sign the immutable SHA. (Claim-gate ref-resolution coherence is a
sibling story; this slice is the attested-signing pin only.)

## What

- Bind the SHA through the EXISTING MANIFEST CHANNEL, NOT a new signed-payload field.
  `signing._canonical_payload` signs exactly `{v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest}` and
  `sign_manifest`/`compute_signature` already sign the full `manifest: list[str]`. Carry the
  pin as a MANIFEST STEP (`verified-at-sha:<sha>`): it enters the signed bytes (bound +
  reproducible) WITHOUT touching `_canonical_payload` or bumping `PAYLOAD_VERSION` — so NO
  invalidation of prior certified closures. The manifest-vs-payload signing decision is
  recorded in the architecture ADR.
- Record `verified_at_sha` as a queryable ticket field (convenience metadata; the trust anchor
  is the manifest step). Keep the existing `head_sha` audit field AS IS (additive).
- Resolution boundary: resolve the client `ref` (default `origin/main`) to an immutable SHA;
  an UNRESOLVABLE ref in attested mode fails closed (no sign).
- Atomicity MECHANISM (named, not just asserted): the combined manifest pin + `verified_at_sha`
  ticket write is serialized through the ticket's existing event-append / optimistic-concurrency
  boundary (compare-and-set on the current SHA), so concurrent retries / races between two
  verifications against different SHAs cannot interleave (no check-then-act TOCTOU). Set-once
  per SHA; re-verifying a DIFFERENT SHA overwrites (no stale/double pin).
- verified_at_sha lifecycle: on REOPEN/abandon the pin is retired (cleared/invalidated) so a
  stale SHA never outlives its closure; local-mode runs never set it.
- Bar signing in `local` source mode: verify + report, NO signature.
- Shape the manifest pin so a future move to DSSE + asymmetric + transparency log is an envelope
  swap, not a rewrite (KEEP HMAC now; limits documented in S6).

## Scope

- In: manifest-channel SHA binding (no payload-version bump), `verified_at_sha` field +
  lifecycle, resolution boundary + fail-closed, atomic/idempotent/overwrite pin with a named
  serialization mechanism, local-mode signing bar, in-toto envelope shape.
- Out: claim-gate ref-resolution coherence (sibling story); snapshot/cache (S1/S2); HMAC-trust
  docs (S6, dependency).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] An attested PASS adds a `verified-at-sha:<sha>` MANIFEST STEP covered by the signature;
      verifying certifies the SHA. No `_canonical_payload` change, NO `PAYLOAD_VERSION` bump
      (regression test: pre-existing signatures still verify). The decision is recorded in the ADR.
- [ ] `verified_at_sha` is a queryable ticket field; re-running against the same SHA reproduces
      a verdict + manifest over identical content; `head_sha` audit field preserved (additive).
- [ ] The pin + field write is serialized via the ticket's event-append/optimistic-concurrency
      (compare-and-set) — proven by a concurrency/retry test (two writers racing, and a re-verify
      against a different SHA overwrites with no stale/double pin); NOT verified by inspection.
- [ ] On reopen/abandon the `verified_at_sha` pin is retired (no stale SHA outlives the closure);
      a `local`-mode run (dirty allowed) never sets it and never emits a signature.
- [ ] An unresolvable ref in attested mode fails closed (no signature).
- [ ] The manifest pin is shaped as an in-toto-style statement so the HMAC envelope can be
      swapped for DSSE/asymmetric later without changing the pin's data shape (covered by a test/contract).
